title: Filling a window-board gap
subtitle: Seal the joint between window board and plaster with filler and caulk
excerpt: How to fill and seal the gap that opens between a window board and the surrounding plaster: backing the deep gap with expanding foam, applying filler to level the surface, finishing with a flexible caulk bead, and painting over.
sourceNotes: Standard UK decorating practice. Collins Complete DIY Manual.
aliases: gap between window sill and plaster

glossaryTerms.definition: A paintable, flexible filler used at junctions between materials that move independently (timber to plaster, window board to wall). Unlike rigid filler, flexible caulk accommodates seasonal movement without cracking.

content.text: The gap that opens between a window board and the surrounding plaster is one of the most common finishing faults in a redecorated room. It appears because the timber window board moves seasonally while the plaster does not. A rigid filler cracks when the movement recurs; the correct fill is a shallow rigid filler layer for appearance, sealed with a flexible
Overpaintable acrylic sealant. Available in 300 ml cartridges; a standard caulking gun accepts the cartridge. Sold as interior filler-caulk or flexible gap filler at builders' merchants.
For filling any deep section of the gap before the caulk coat.
For any gap deeper than 10 mm that would take excessive filler to fill solid.
Remove any old filler, caulk, or paint that has bridged the gap by picking and cutting it clear. Vacuum out the dust. A clean gap bonds better than a packed one.

If the gap is deeper than 10 mm, squeeze a small amount of expanding PU foam into the void (not more than a quarter fill; it expands to several times its wet volume). Allow to cure, then slice back flush with a craft knife.
Step 3, apply filler for surface continuity.

If the gap is visible as a step between the window board and the plaster, apply lightweight filler to the plaster side only, feathering it out from the board edge. Do not pack filler into the joint itself. Allow to dry and sand flush.

Gun a small bead of flexible caulk into the joint between the window board and the plaster. Run a wet finger along the bead to press it into the joint and leave a neat, slightly concave profile. Wipe away any excess with a damp cloth. Allow to skin fully (at minimum 2 hours) before painting.
Once the caulk is fully cured (24 hours), it can be overpainted with standard emulsion or gloss. Caulk applied to unpainted surfaces needs painting; it yellows and shrinks slightly if left exposed.

attrs.cause: Too much caulk was applied in a single bead, or the gap is too wide for caulk alone.
attrs.fix: Cut out the old caulk, back-fill the void with backer rod (a foam cylinder that fills the gap and gives the caulk a suitable depth), then apply a fresh caulk bead over the backer rod.

attrs.symptom: Rigid filler in the joint is cracking repeatedly
attrs.cause: The window board is moving; rigid filler cannot flex with it.
attrs.fix: Remove all rigid filler from the joint. Replace with flexible caulk only.
